content.title: Further Evolution of the ACC/AHA Clinical Practice Guideline Recommendation Classification System
content.introduction: For 3 decades, the American College of Cardiology (ACC) and the American Heart Association (AHA) have jointly developed clinical practice guidelines to align patient care with scientific evidence. The 2015 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Management of Patients With Supraventricular Tachycardia introduced the latest recommendation classification system, which continues to evolve.
content.classes_of_recommendation: Guideline recommendations are categorized by the Class of Recommendation (COR) and Level of Evidence (LOE). Class I recommendations are strong, indicating usefulness and effectiveness. Class II recommendations are weaker.
authors: Jonathan L. Halperin, MD, FACC, FAHA
Glenn N. Levine, MD, FACC, FAHA
Sana M. Al-Khatib, MD, MHS, FACC, FAHA
Biykem Bozkurt, MD, PhD, FACC, FAHA
Ralph G. Brindis, MD, MPH, MACC
Lee A. Fleisher, MD, FACC, FAHA
Susan J. Pressler, PhD, RN, FAHA

content.Title: ACC/AHA Recommendation System: Applying Class of Recommendation and Level of Evidence to Clinical Strategies, Interventions, Treatments, or Diagnostic Testing in Patient Care

Suggested Phrases: Treatment/strategy A is recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B
Treatment A should be chosen over treatment B
Treatment/strategy A is probably recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B
It is reasonable to choose treatment A over treatment B
Usefulness/effectiveness is unknown/unclear/uncertain or not well established

Class III (No Benefit - Moderate).Context: Generally, LOE A or B use only

Description: High-quality evidence from more than 1 RCT
One or more RCTs corroborated by high-quality registry studies
Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more RCTs
Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more well-designed, well-executed nonrandomized studies, observational studies, or registry studies
Randomized or nonrandomized observational or registry studies with limitations of design or execution
Physiological or mechanistic studies in human subjects
Consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience

content.guideline: The guideline discusses the classification of evidence levels in clinical practice, specifically the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association's system. Levels of Evidence (LOE) A, B, and C are detailed, with an emphasis on adjusting recommendations based on evidence strength. It highlights the integration of cost and health economics, the creation of decision tools, and the incorporation of electronic health records. The guideline seeks to enhance the precision, granularity, and applicability of recommendations.
content.context: The guideline aligns with recommendations by the Institute of Medicine in 2011, addressing challenges like evidence quality assessment and cost integration. It stresses ongoing adaptations to support comprehensive evidence evaluation.
